title: Plastic waste generation by region and polymer
subtitle: Polymer types are as follows: Acrylonitrile Butadiene (AB), Acrylonitrile Styrene Acrylate (ASA), Styrene Acrylonitrile (SAN), Polyurethane (PUR), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE).
caption:
Plastic waste generation by region and polymer
Polymer types are as follows: Acrylonitrile Butadiene (AB), Acrylonitrile Styrene Acrylate (ASA), Styrene Acrylonitrile (SAN), Polyurethane (PUR), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE).

This stacked horizontal bar chart displays the regional composition of plastic waste generation for thirteen distinct polymer categories in 2019, with each bar representing 100% of the global waste for that specific material type. The horizontal axis measures the percentage share, ranging from 0% to 100%, while the vertical axis lists polymer types including PVC, PUR, PS, HDPE, PET, PP, LDPE, LLDPE, and others. The legend identifies ten geographic regions, including Europe, China, Asia (excluding China and India), the United States, and the Americas (excluding USA). Across nearly all polymer types, the United States, shown in teal, consistently occupies the largest single share of the bar, typically extending from the right edge to cover roughly 20% to 25% of the total length. Europe, represented in blue, forms the second largest consistent block on the left side of most bars, generally accounting for approximately 20% of the total. China, shown in maroon, and Asia excluding China and India, shown in green, follow as significant contributors, often filling the middle sections of the bars. For specific polymers like Road marking coatings, the United States share appears visually larger than for other categories, occupying nearly a third of the bar. Conversely, for Fibres, the share attributed to Asia excluding China and India appears slightly more prominent relative to other regions compared to polymers like PVC. The data aggregates regional totals based on OECD specifications to show how different parts of the world contribute to the global waste stream for each material. The visual pattern shows the United States and Europe as the dominant sources of waste across the majority of polymer types listed.
